policy_statement: Apply a purchasing framework and build a regional network to increase institutional and corporate procurement of pro-climate, pro-health food options, including certifications and a purchasing/distribution network.
mechanism_description: Creating procurement standards and distribution networks increases institutional purchasing of lower-emissions food, shifting demand and supply toward climate-friendly production and reducing food-system greenhouse gas emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Procurement frameworks that explicitly include minimizing food waste and improving purchasing/menu planning reduce avoidable food demand and upstream resource inputs (land, water, fertilizer, energy), lowering material/energy throughput per meal served.
primary_causal_pathway: Institutional purchasing standards + aggregated demand → shift in food types/practices (↓ ruminant products, ↑ plant-based/regenerative/verified supply) → ↓ lifecycle food-system GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O, CO₂) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Public and private institutions (schools, hospitals, city facilities, large employers) adopt procurement standards, certifications, and supplier/distribution networks that prioritize “pro-climate” foods and suppliers.  
(2) Mediating process: Large-buyer demand reshapes markets and supply chains—altering production practices (e.g., less emissions-intensive farming, fertilizer/manure management), product mix (more plant-forward), and logistics—leading to lower emissions intensities and/or lower-emissions foods being produced and purchased.  
(3) Outcome: Reduced lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions from the food system, especially methane (livestock), nitrous oxide (soils/fertilizer), and CO₂ (energy/land-use-related components depending on sourcing).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions from food production and associated supply chains by shifting institutional purchasing toward lower-emissions food options.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement standards shift demand toward lower-emissions foods and practices, yielding net reductions in food-system GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy can improve health, equity, and resilience, the mechanism as stated is explicitly designed to reduce food-system emissions via demand/supply-chain transformation; that is the largest climate-relevant effect across instances.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s):  
   - Resource Efficiency: When the framework includes minimizing food waste (common/included in the mechanism description), it reduces wasted food procurement and upstream resource use per service delivered.  
4) Edge cases applied: If a procurement framework were “local/organic” without emissions safeguards, impacts could vary; however this canonical mechanism is explicitly “pro-climate” and aimed at emissions reduction, so mitigation remains dominant.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy implementing this mechanism (pro-climate procurement standards/networks to drive lower-emissions purchasing) will primarily target GHG reductions; resource-efficiency co-benefits are inherent when waste minimization is a built-in component of the framework.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a specific implementation focuses only on “local” sourcing without lifecycle GHG criteria, emissions could increase (e.g., inefficient production/transport); similarly, not all procurement frameworks inherently reduce food waste unless explicitly included—here it is part of the described mechanism, supporting Resource Efficiency as secondary.
key_indicators: “Support institutional food purchasing”; “Apply a purchasing framework”; “bolster institutional and corporate food procurement”; “proclimate, pro-health options”; “support supply-chain infrastructure”; “build a regional food system network”
co_benefits: Public health/nutrition benefits (“pro-health” foods); local economic development/supply-chain resilience from regional network and infrastructure; potential food access improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an enabling component (“support supply-chain infrastructure”/network-building) that could be seen as infrastructure/planning, but the dominant policy lever described is institutional procurement via a purchasing framework.
